subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear aim stated that distinguishes imagery vs rehearsal.
Key elements of Baddeley & Hitchâs model identified and linked to conditions.
Both null and alternative hypotheses correctly identify IV and DV.
weaknesses: Relevance of the investigation is cursory and lacks depth.
Theory description is simplistic and lacks empirical citations.
Variables are not operationalized with sufficient precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strand sits in the 3â4 band but at the lower end. The aim, model link and hypothesis are present and correct, yet explanations are shallow. Thus, a mid-band score of 3 accurately reflects performance.

strengths: Independentâmeasures design is justified with reference to demand characteristics and practice effects.
Opportunity sampling choice and participant characteristics are explained.
Controlled variables and materials are described with brief reasons for standardization.
weaknesses: Materials selection criteria and randomization method are not detailed.
Potential biases of opportunity sampling are not addressed.
Environmental controls beyond time of day are omitted.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the âexplainedâ descriptor (band 3â4) but lack comprehensive detail, placing the criterion at the lower end of its range (3/4).

strengths: Graphical presentation addresses the hypothesis with correct technique.
Both descriptive and inferential statistics are applied.
Findings are linked to the hypothesis direction in interpretation.
weaknesses: Axes labels are incomplete and error bars undefined.
p-value is misinterpreted (p=.16 treated as significant).
Confusion between parametric and non-parametric tests undermines accuracy.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student attempts appropriate statistics and graphical work but with technical errors and misinterpretations. This aligns with the 3â4 band at its lower end (3/6).

strengths: Findings are discussed with reference to the visuospatial sketchpad and phonological loop.
Strengths and limitations are directly relevant and tied to validity/bias concerns.
Proposed modifications are explicitly linked to stated limitations.
weaknesses: Discussion of theory is descriptive rather than critically analytical.
Some strengths/limitations are repetitive and lack empirical support.
Modifications could be more fully justified with implementation details.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All strands meet the 3â4 band descriptors and the modifications strand shows clear linkage to limitations. Performance is at the upper end of mid-band, justifying a score of 4/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph with error bars is included and clearly contrasts the two conditions, so it addresses the hypothesis. However, axes lack full labels/units and error bars are not defined; these technical flaws match the âgraph addresses hypothesis but contains errorsâ descriptor, earning 3 marks.

Findings are stated (image mean 10.17 vs word 8.5) and an interpretation is offered, but the student incorrectly concludes the difference is significant despite p =.16 and cites p<0.5. Misinterpretation undermines the link to the hypothesis. This falls into the 1-2 band; some linkage exists, so 2 marks are given.

Both null and alternative hypotheses are written and the IV (imagery vs word condition) and DV (number of items recalled) are correctly identified. Neither variable is clearly operationalised (e.g., â15 colour images of concrete nounsâ or precise scoring rules), so the work matches the 3-4 band, lower mark.

Opportunity sampling is identified and the choice of high-school students (age, language, convenience, ethics) is explained. The rationale for limiting age and language shows an attempt to justify the choice, so the criterion for âexplainedâ is met, albeit briefly. A 3 is awarded.

Baddeley & Hitchâs model is outlined with the central executive, phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad named and briefly described. The student then links imagery to the visuospatial sketchpad and rehearsal to the phonological loop, thereby connecting the model to the planned comparison. The link is present but thin, lacking discussion of capacity limits or dual-task evidence, so the descriptor for âdescribed and link explainedâ is only partially met. A mid-band (3) is therefore appropriate.

The student relates the higher image scores to the visuospatial sketchpad and discusses relative capacities of the two subsystems, thereby referencing the background model. The discussion is mostly descriptive and not deeply critical, so it meets the 3-4 band but not the higher band. A score of 3 is appropriate.

The student states an explicit aim â to examine whether imagery enhances free-recall compared with rehearsal â and briefly comments that this is relevant to understanding working-memory processes. However, the explanation of why this matters (e.g., educational implications or theoretical gaps) is cursory. The clarity and basic relevance push the work above a mere statement, but the limited depth prevents full explanation. This aligns best with the 3-4 band and merits the lower end of that range.

Strengths and limitations for sampling, procedure and design are listed and their impact on validity or bias is described, demonstrating relevance to the investigation. Explanations are present but not thoroughly developed, matching the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

Controlled variables (time of day, equal exposure, standardized video) and materials (random word list, equal image/word counts) are mentioned with short reasons (to avoid fatigue, correlation, standardisation). The explanation goes beyond naming, so the 3-4 band is justified; depth is limited, hence 3 marks.

The student proposes using a more diverse sample and measuring individual cognitive abilities, explicitly tying these suggestions to stated limitations of convenience sampling and individual differences. Justification is present but concise, satisfying âlinked to limitationsâ but not âfully justifiedâ at depth required for 5-6, so 3 marks are awarded.

The student states they used an independent-measures design and justifies it by referring to demand characteristics, practice effects and testing time. That constitutes an explanation rather than a mere description; however, discussion is brief and lacks potential weaknesses (e.g., individual differences). This fits the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

Means, standard deviations and an independent-samples t-test (or z/Mann-Whitney) are reported. Nonetheless, the p-value is misinterpreted (p =.16 reported as significant) and there is confusion between parametric and non-parametric tests. Because both descriptive and inferential statistics are attempted but contain errors, the work fits the 3-4 band, lower mark.

annotations.comment: Procedure strength notes random allocation but does not specify how participants were randomized. Detail the randomization process to enhance procedural transparency.
The conclusion on educational implications is brief. Expand on how findings could inform specific teaching strategies or memory interventions.

The aim is stated (âto explore how imagery affects memory retention and free recallâ) but relevance to theoretical gaps or practical applications is minimal. Expand on why understanding imageryâs impact matters for educational or cognitive theory.
The working memory definition is colloquial (âlike sticky notesâ) and lacks precision. Adopt a formal operational definition and integrate empirical citations to strengthen academic tone.
Several paragraphs restate the studyâs aim separately. Combine them into a concise introduction that presents aim, variables, and relevance in one coherent section.

The description of the central executive is unclear (âdirects the claimâ¦â). Clarify the functions of the central executive and its control of slave systems to accurately reflect Baddeley & Hitchâs model.
The null hypothesis is directional for a oneâtailed test but not specified. Clarify whether it is nonâdirectional (twoâtailed) or directional to match later statistical testing.
The alternative hypothesis repeats âimage and word condition in image and word condition.â Simplify and correct redundancy for clarity.
IV and DV are named correctly but not operationalized. Specify how âimagery conditionâ and ânumber of items recalledâ were operationally defined (e.g., number of images shown, scoring rules).
Materials are generically described as ârandom words and imagesâ without selection criteria. Specify how stimuli were chosen, randomized, and pre-tested for familiarity.
Procedure lists informed consent and debriefing but omits how random allocation was implemented. Describe the randomization method (e.g., draw names, random number generator).
The independentâmeasures design rationale mentions demand characteristics but omits individual differences as a limitation. Acknowledge and discuss how participant variability might affect results.
Opportunity sampling is described but its potential for sampling bias is not addressed here. Explain why this method was chosen and how bias was mitigated.
Participantsâ age and ethnicity are listed, but justification focuses on English fluency only. Clarify why language fluency and age range were prioritized over demographic diversity.
Control of time of day is described; however, other environmental factors (e.g., noise, lighting) are not mentioned. Include additional controls for external validity.
The analysis introduction (âwe analyzed the correct number of responsesâ) is vague. State which descriptive and inferential statistics were applied.
The results table presents means and SDs but lacks a descriptive title and clear heading alignment. Include comprehensive table titles and define all abbreviations.
Consider reporting an effect size (e.g., Cohenâs d) alongside p-values to illustrate the practical significance of the imagery versus word conditions.
Discussion links findings to the visuospatial sketchpad but lacks critical analysis of alternative explanations. Engage with literature on dual-task interference and capacity limits.
The bar graph lacks fully labelled axes (units, condition names) and error bars are undefined. Add clear axis titles and specify what error bars represent.
Interpretation of mean differences describes magnitude but does not link back to hypothesis direction. Explicitly connect whether imagery performed better as predicted.
The p-value threshold is misâstated (p<0.5) and p=0.1615 is incorrectly deemed significant. Use the conventional p<0.05 criterion and correct the interpretation.
Strengths and limitations of convenience sampling are discussed separately across pages. Consolidate these points to improve coherence and avoid redundancy.
The description of subsystem capacities (âvisuospatial has larger capacityâ) is asserted without empirical backing. Cite studies quantifying these capacities for support.
Limitations mention individual differences but do not link to a concrete modification. Propose including a pre-test cognitive ability measure to control for this confound.
The repeated section on independent design strengths duplicates earlier discussion. Merge these to streamline evaluation of research design.
Limitation of independent design acknowledges non-equivalence of groups but misses matched-pairs alternative. Suggest considering a matched-pairs design to reduce group bias.
Modifications propose a more diverse sample but lack specifics. Outline recruitment strategies (e.g., stratified sampling) to achieve representativeness.
